common_name: Adams Elderberry (Classic High-Yield Large-Cluster American Elderberry Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Adams is one of the best pollinators for Bob Gordon and Nova elderberry cultivars — planting two or more different cultivars consistently produces 30–50% heavier fruit crops than single-variety plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Adams is one of the oldest and most widely established named elderberry cultivars — selected from wild American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) populations and long used as the benchmark for comparison in elderberry cultivar trials. It produces large, flat-topped flower clusters (cymes) in June–July — among the largest of named elderberry cultivars — followed by heavy, pendant clusters of small, dark purple-black berries in late August–September. Adams is valued not only for its direct productivity but as an exceptionally effective cross-pollinator for other cultivars: planting Adams near Bob Gordon, Nova, or other cultivars consistently boosts overall production across the planting. The heavy, well-formed berry clusters of Adams are well-suited to all standard elderberry processing: elderberry syrup (a popular immune support product), elderberry wine, elderberry jelly, and elderflower preparations from the spring flowers. The large flower clusters are among the most attractive of any elderberry cultivar for elderflower cordials, fritters, and other culinary uses. As with all American elderberry, Adams spreads vigorously by root suckers and can naturalize in favorable moist conditions — manage the suckering to maintain a defined shrub rather than a spreading thicket. In zone 9, elderberry performs best in the slightly cooler, moister areas — near water features or in low spots where soil moisture is consistent.

common_name: Butterfly Pink Pentas (Heat-Proof Soft-Pink Star Cluster Hummingbird Annual)

planting.note: Plant container transplants after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; the soft pink color of Butterfly Pink is more versatile in mixed planting combinations than the deep red — it complements both warm and cool color companions in summer borders.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Butterfly Pink is a soft rose-pink to warm-pink cultivar in the Butterfly series of Pentas lanceolata — producing the same rounded star-cluster flower heads and outstanding heat tolerance as the series, in a color that is more softly toned than the vivid deep red of 'Butterfly Deep Red'. The warm soft-pink color is among the most versatile for mixed summer combinations: it complements orange and yellow tropicals without the color clash that can occur with cool pink, works well with deep purple companions (sweet potato vine, Purple Queen Bougainvillea), and creates elegant pastels when combined with white and lavender. Like all Pentas, Butterfly Pink is a butterfly and hummingbird magnet throughout its extended bloom season — the nectar-rich star-shaped flower clusters are visited by swallowtails, monarchs, gulf fritillaries, skippers, and other pollinators in large numbers. The heat performance in Gulf South summer conditions is outstanding — one of the primary ornamental assets of the genus. In zone 10-12, Pentas grows as a perennial subshrub. In zone 9, it functions as a warm-season annual that may self-sow in sheltered conditions. Mass planting in 12-18 inch grids creates the most effective nectar resource for migrating monarchs passing through the Gulf South in September–October.

common_name: Super Blue Liriope (Bold Deep-Blue Flowering Lilyturf for Shade Borders)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Super Blue's heavy flowering is maintained in partial shade — one of the few ornamental groundcovers that produces significant flower display without requiring full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Super Blue is a Liriope muscari cultivar selected for exceptionally heavy and prolific flowering — producing dense spikes of rich lavender-blue to deep violet-blue flowers in August–September well above the dark-green foliage, creating one of the best flower displays of any commonly grown liriope. The flower spike quality distinguishes Super Blue from standard liriope varieties: the spikes are longer, more densely flowered, and more vivid in color than typical Liriope muscari cultivars, creating a stronger ornamental statement in the late-summer border when few other shade perennials are actively flowering. Liriope is one of the most important and versatile groundcover plants for Gulf South landscapes — tolerating the full range of conditions from full sun to deep shade, from clay soils to sandy soils, from moist to periodically drought-stressed, and from salt spray to inland heat. Super Blue is an outstanding choice for border edges, the fronts of foundation plantings, and mass groundcover plantings in partial-shade positions under trees and along shaded building foundations. The dark-green, strap-shaped leaves provide clean, year-round evergreen structure even when the plant is not in bloom. Black berries persist through fall and winter, providing interest and food for small birds. Divide established clumps every 3–5 years when they become overcrowded and productivity declines.

common_name: Sunstorm Coral Vinca (Heat-Proof Coral-Orange Madagascar Periwinkle Annual)

planting.note: Plant container transplants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Vinca requires good soil drainage — plants in poorly drained soils develop Phytophthora root rot rapidly; raised beds and well-amended garden beds provide the drainage needed for reliable performance.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sunstorm Coral is a coral-orange to warm-peach-orange Catharanthus roseus (Madagascar periwinkle, vinca) — one of the most important and reliable summer annuals for Gulf South full-sun gardens, blooming prolifically and continuously through the brutal July–August heat that collapses most other summer annuals. Catharanthus roseus is native to Madagascar and specifically adapted to hot, humid conditions — its heat performance in Gulf South summers is among the best of any commonly grown annual. The Sunstorm series was bred for improved heat and humidity tolerance, early and continuous flowering, and mounding, well-branched plant habit. The warm coral-orange color of 'Sunstorm Coral' creates a vivid summer display that works with both warm tropical companions (lantana, blue salvia) and cooler purple and chartreuse foliage plants. The waxy, glossy leaves and five-petaled, flat-faced flowers are characteristic of the species. Deadheading is not required — vinca naturally drops spent flowers and produces new blooms continuously. Good drainage is the critical cultural requirement — Phytophthora root rot in poorly drained soil is the primary cause of vinca failure in Gulf South gardens. Vinca is toxic if ingested and contains alkaloids with significant pharmacological properties that have been the basis for cancer chemotherapy drugs (vincristine, vinblastine).

common_name: Pineapple Quince (Fragrant Sweet Large-Fruited Quince for Gulf South Preserves)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pineapple Quince begins bearing fruit in 2–4 years from planting and reaches peak production by year 5–7; it is self-fertile but may produce larger fruits with cross-pollination from another quince variety.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pineapple Quince is the most popular and widely grown quince cultivar in North America — producing large, round to pyriform, golden-yellow fruits with a distinctive sweet, pineapple-like fragrance and flavor that sets it apart from tarter, more typical quince. Quince (Cydonia oblonga) is one of the oldest cultivated fruits, native to the Caucasus region and grown throughout the Mediterranean for millennia — the golden fruit of ancient mythology may have been a quince rather than an apple. The raw fruit is astringent and usually not eaten fresh, but becomes transformed by cooking: quince turns a beautiful ruby-red to deep rose color when cooked slowly with sugar, developing a rich, sweet, floral flavor for quince paste (membrillo), quince jelly, and quince preserves that are among the most distinctive of tree-fruit preparations. 'Pineapple' is selected for sweeter, less astringent raw flavor than most quince — it can be eaten fresh when fully ripe (golden-yellow with red cheeks), though cooking still brings out the most complex flavor. The spring bloom — large, cup-shaped, pale pink to white flowers — is ornamentally beautiful and provides excellent bee forage. In zone 9, quince performs best in the cooler, northern parts of zone 9 (zone 9a) where it receives adequate winter chilling (150+ hours below 45°F); southern zone 9 may provide insufficient chilling for reliable fruiting.
